does: This paper presents a practical playbook developed by OpenAI for conducting third-party evaluations of frontier AI systems. It provides structured guidance on assessing model capabilities across various domains, evaluating the effectiveness of safety safeguards, and validating evaluation methodologies themselves. The playbook establishes concrete frameworks and best practices for what evaluators should measure, how to design rigorous tests, and what standards constitute valid assessment procedures. By sharing OpenAI's accumulated experience in external evaluations, the work makes evaluation methodology explicit and reproducible, offering actionable recommendations that independent researchers and organizations can apply when assessing advanced AI systems.
connects: This work builds on the growing emphasis within the AI safety and ML community on independent evaluation as a governance mechanism for frontier models. It responds to calls for transparency and external scrutiny that have intensified following concerns about rapid model deployment without adequate assessment. The playbook extends prior work on AI benchmarking and evaluation frameworks, learning from established practices in third-party safety audits and red-teaming approaches. It also engages with broader discussions about alignment, capability assessment methods, and the role of external validation in building trust—connecting to prior efforts by various organizations to establish evaluation standards and adversarial testing protocols for large language models.
enables: This shared playbook can accelerate the professionalization and standardization of AI evaluation practices across the industry and research community. By making evaluation approaches transparent and reproducible, it enables more consistent assessment of frontier systems and supports comparative analysis across different model developers. The guidance can help regulators, safety researchers, and independent auditors establish baseline competencies and methodologies, potentially facilitating regulatory frameworks that rely on third-party evaluation. Future work can build on these foundations to develop more sophisticated evaluation metrics, improve validity assessment techniques, and create agreed-upon standards that span multiple organizations, ultimately supporting more trustworthy development and deployment of advanced AI systems.
